Anatomy · Deep Cervical Anatomy

Cervical Fascia & Neck Triangles

Fascial layers · Carotid sheath · Danger space · Triangle borders & deep contents

Overview Fascial Layers Triangles Quick Review
1

Big-Picture Organization

Surface map
Ant + Post
Two major cervical triangles
Post triangle split by
Omohyoid
→ Occipital + Supraclavicular
Fascia pattern
4 layers
Investing → Pretracheal → Carotid sheath → Prevertebral
Carotid sheath ⭐
IJV · CN X · CCA
Lateral → medial
Framework: Superficial fascia carries the platysma. Deep fascia wraps muscle groups and viscera into compartments. Triangles give you a surface map to locate what lies beneath.
2

Cervical Fascial Layers

Think of the neck as concentric rings of fascia — each ring compartmentalizes a specific set of structures and creates potential spaces relevant to infection and surgery.

Superficial fascia

Superficial Cervical Fascia

Subcutaneous layer attached to the dermis. Contains:

  • Platysma muscle
  • Cutaneous nerves and vessels
  • Lymphatics
Deep — Layer 1

Investing Layer of Deep Cervical Fascia

Encircles the entire neck; attaches to the nuchal ligament posteriorly. Encloses SCM and trapezius. The accessory nerve (CN XI) travels in this region.

Deep — Layer 2

Middle (Pretracheal) Layer

  • Muscular part — encloses the infrahyoid (strap) muscles
  • Visceral part — encloses the trachea and esophagus
  • Merges laterally with the carotid sheath
Deep — Layer 3

Prevertebral Fascia

Sheaths the vertebral column muscles. Anteriorly invests the longus and scalene muscles; posteriorly wraps the paraspinal muscles. Attaches laterally to transverse processes.

Carotid sheath ⭐

Visceral Fascia + Carotid Sheath

The lateral extensions of visceral fascia form the carotid sheath. Contents from lateral → medial:

  • Internal jugular vein (IJV)
  • Vagus nerve (CN X)
  • Common carotid artery (CCA)
⚠ Danger space

Retropharyngeal Danger Space

Interval behind the pharynx, between the carotid sheaths. Extends to approximately T4. Infection here can spread rapidly along fascial planes into the mediastinum.

3

Superficial Muscular Triangles

For each triangle: learn the borders first, then the major deep structures you expect to find underneath.

Anterior Cervical Triangle — 4 Subdivisions

Anterior

Submental Triangle

  • Borders: Ant. bellies of both digastrics + hyoid body
  • Floor: mylohyoid
  • Submental lymph nodes
  • Anterior jugular vein tributaries
Anterior

Submandibular Triangle

  • Borders: Mandible + ant. & post. bellies of digastric
  • Submandibular gland
  • Facial artery & vein
  • CN XII (hypoglossal), nerve to mylohyoid
  • Regional lymph nodes
Anterior

Carotid Triangle ⭐

  • Borders: SCM + post. belly digastric + omohyoid
  • Carotid sheath (IJV, CN X, CCA)
  • Carotid bifurcation + sinus/body
  • CN XII, ansa cervicalis
  • Deep cervical lymph nodes
Anterior

Muscular Triangle

  • Borders: SCM + omohyoid + midline
  • Infrahyoid (strap) muscles
  • Thyroid & parathyroid glands
  • Larynx, trachea, esophagus

Posterior Cervical Triangle — 2 Subdivisions

Dividing landmark: The omohyoid splits the posterior triangle into occipital (upper) and supraclavicular (lower).
Posterior

Occipital Triangle

  • Borders: Post. SCM + ant. trapezius + omohyoid
  • CN XI (accessory nerve) — injury → shoulder drop
  • Cervical plexus branches
  • Upper brachial plexus, supraclavicular nerve
Posterior

Supraclavicular Triangle

  • Borders: Post. SCM + omohyoid + clavicle
  • Subclavian artery
  • Brachial plexus trunks
  • Nerve to subclavius
  • Lymph nodes (Virchow's node region)
Clinical use: Use the anterior triangles for the carotid pulse, thyroid anatomy, and submandibular gland. Use the posterior triangles for CN XI injury, subclavian access, and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
4

Quick Review — One-Pass Memory Check

Click to reveal. Get these cold before lab or a quiz.

1
Which fascial layer contains the platysma?▾
Superficial cervical fascia (subcutaneous layer).
2
Which deep layer encloses the SCM and trapezius?▾
The investing layer — superficial layer of deep cervical fascia.
3
What are the carotid sheath contents (lateral → medial)?▾
Internal jugular vein → Vagus nerve (CN X) → Common carotid artery.
4
What is the danger space and where does it extend?▾
Retropharyngeal interval between the carotid sheaths, extending to ~T4. Infection here spreads rapidly to the mediastinum.
5
What divides the posterior triangle into two?▾
The omohyoid muscle — into occipital triangle (upper) and supraclavicular triangle (lower).
6
Which triangle is richest in carotid structures?▾
Carotid triangle — overlies the carotid bifurcation, carotid sinus/body, IJV, CN X, and CN XII.
7
Where is CN XI found, and what happens if it's damaged?▾
Occipital triangle (posterior). Damage → trapezius weakness → drooping shoulder, inability to shrug.
8
What major vessel is in the supraclavicular triangle?▾
Subclavian artery (plus brachial plexus trunks and the nerve to subclavius).
✕
